coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted array discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds and colorimetric−fluorescence dual-mode sensor for detection of 1,4-benzenedithiol based on peroxidase-like nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Linchun
Jiang, Liushan
Li, Shuangying
Song, Denghao
Dong, Guangyu
Bu, Lutong
Chen, Chunmao
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Present work reported a novel nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs with excellent peroxidase-like activity obtained by loading Cu and N co-doped carbon dots on g-C3N4 (graphitic carbon nitride). g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs can catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radical •OH, which oxidizes o-phenylenediamine to 2,3-diaminophenazine, which emits orange fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation. The experimental results confirmed that 1,4-benzenedithiol (BDT) could inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs. Based the principle above, a colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor for rapidly sensing of BDT was creatively constructed with assisting of a smartphone. The sensor showed excellent linearity over ranges of 0.75–132 μM and 0.33–60.0 μM with detection limits of 0.32 μM and 0.25 μM for colorimetric and fluorescence detection, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric array sensor was constructed to distinguish six sulfur-containing compounds according to the difference in the degree of inhibition of nanozyme activity by different sulfur-containing compounds. The array sensor could distinguish sulfur-containing compounds at low concentration as low as 0.4 μM. The results validated that the designed sensor was a convenient and fast platform, which could be utilized as a reliably portable tool for the efficient and accurate detection of BDT and the discrimination of multiple sulfur compounds in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur-containing compounds
Nanozymes
Colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds with smartphone
Peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs and the principle of colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT
Steady-state kinetic assay of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs
Colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT by spectroscopy
Colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT with smartphone
Detection of BDT in real samples
Visual colorimetric discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) TEM images and (D) EDS spectrum of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, (E) XRD spectra of Cu, N-CDs, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, (F) XPS spectra of Cu, N-CDs, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs.
(A) Full-scan spectrum of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 2s, (C) N 1s and (D) Cu 2p.

(A) UV–vis spectra of (a) OPD + H2O2, (b) g-C3N4 + OPD + H2O2, (c) Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2, (d) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, (e) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2; (B) UV–vis spectra of (a) OPD + H2O2, (b) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD, (c) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD H2O2, (d) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2 + BDT.

3D contour photo of (A) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, (B) OPD, (C) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD, (D) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2, (E) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2 + 16.7 μM BDT, (F) g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2 + 167 μM BDT (100 μL of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs (1 mg/mL), 100 μL of OPD (4 mM), and 600 μL of H2O2 (10 mM). The pH value of NaAc-HAc buffer was 6.5, the reaction was carried out at 25 °C for 30 min. Ex = 250 nm, the photovoltage was controlled at 650V).

(A) Absorption spectra of the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD system incubated with different concentrations of BDT in the range of 0.75–151 μM. (B) The linear relationship between the absorbance at 425 nm (A 425nm) and the concentration of BDT. (C) Fluorescence spectra of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD system incubated with various concentrations of BDT in the range of 0.33 − 60.0 μM. (D) The linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 585 nm and 362 nm (F 585 nm/F 362 nm) and the concentration of BDT.

(A) Calibration curve of BDT in visual colorimetric mode. Inset: naked-eye images of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD system with BDT in the range of 0.75–132 μM. (B) Calibration curve of BDT in the fluorescence colorimetric mode. Inset: fluorescence images of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD system with BDT in the range of 0.33–60.0 μM.
Schematic illustration of smartphone-assisted visual identification of six sulfur-containing compounds (each, 40 μM).

2D canonical score plots of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs based array sensing system for sulfur-containing compounds at (A) 40 μM, (B) 4 μM, (C) 0.4 μM, (D) 0.1 μM. The hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) tree diagram of the response of six sulfur-containing compounds by using the between-groups linkage method and Squared Euclidean distance at (E) 40 μM, (F) 4 μM, (G) 0.4 μM, (H) 0.1 μM.
The principle of colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode measurement of BDT and the array sensor to distinguish six sulfur-containing compounds.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted array discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds and colorimetric−fluorescence dual-mode sensor for detection of 1,4-benzenedithiol based on peroxidase-like nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Present work reported a novel nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs with excellent peroxidase-like activity obtained by loading Cu and N co-doped carbon dots on g-C3N4 (graphitic carbon nitride). g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs can catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radical •OH, which oxidizes o-phenylenediamine to 2,3-diaminophenazine, which emits orange fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation. The experimental results confirmed that 1,4-benzenedithiol (BDT) could inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs. Based the principle above, a colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor for rapidly sensing of BDT was creatively constructed with assisting of a smartphone. The sensor showed excellent linearity over ranges of 0.75–132 μM and 0.33–60.0 μM with detection limits of 0.32 μM and 0.25 μM for colorimetric and fluorescence detection, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric array sensor was constructed to distinguish six sulfur-containing compounds according to the difference in the degree of inhibition of nanozyme activity by different sulfur-containing compounds. The array sensor could distinguish sulfur-containing compounds at low concentration as low as 0.4 μM. The results validated that the designed sensor was a convenient and fast platform, which could be utilized as a reliably portable tool for the efficient and accurate detection of BDT and the discrimination of multiple sulfur compounds in real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur usually exists as various sulfur-containing compounds in nature. Sulfur-containing compounds are widely used in chemical industry, medicine, agriculture and other aspects [1,2]. However, most sulfur-containing compounds are usually toxic, odorous, and corrosive, causing serious harm to environment and human health [3–5]. Long-term exposure to sulfides at low levels may cause chronic diseases of the blood, skin, eyes, digestive and respiratory systems, and high concentrations can seriously affect the human nervous system [6–8]. Up to now, a series of detection techniques have been established for the determination of sulfur-containing compounds, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], electrochemical method [10,11], chemiluminescence (CL) [12] and so on. However, these methods have the disadvantages of long response time, complex operation, and inapplicability to on-site detection, which greatly limits their practical application. Hence, it is of momentous value to construct an efficient and portable sensor suitable for on-site rapid detection.

In recent years, colorimetric method has been widely used in many fields such as clinical medicine, agriculture and pollutant detection ascribing to its advantages of simpleness, speediness and being economical [13–15]. Enzymatic method has been widely concerned in colorimetry due to its high selectivity and high efficiency. However, the properties of natural enzymes are unstable and susceptible to deterioration or even inactivation due to the influence of external environment [16,17]. Therefore, it is very important to find alternatives with stable properties and natural enzyme activity. Yan et al. firstly discovered the inherent peroxidase activity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in 2007, which opened a new direction in environmental analytical chemistry, since then hundreds of nanomaterials have been continuously discovered to have enzyme-like activity [18,19]. Therefore, nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity are defined as nanozymes [20,21]. Nanozymes usually can accelerate the oxidation of chromogenic substrates such as 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), making them becoming blue, yellow and blue, respectively [22,23]. According to the color change of the sample solution caused by the oxidation process of the chromogenic substrate, visual colorimetric sensors were constructed for rapid detection of pollutants such as phenols [24], antibiotics [25] and pesticides [26]. Zeng et al. prepared Pd and Fe co-doped GeO2 nanozymes with high oxidase-like activity, which can rapidly oxidize TMB. The hydrogen bond between simazine and TMB can lead to the blue light of the solution. Based on this principle, a sensor for sensitive detection of simazine was constructed [27]. Liao et al. synthesized a porous organic polymer of iron porphyrin (Fe-PPOP) with high oxidase-like activity by Friedel-Crafts reaction. Nanozymes oxidize colorless TMB to blue oxTMB mainly through •O2 − radicals, and the reducibility of S2− can inhibit the oxidation process of TMB, resulting in the gradually changing of the solution color from blue to colorless. Based on the above principle, a sensitive colorimetric sensor was developed for the detection of S2− [28]. However, single-mode detection may cause certain errors in on-site detection due to the influence of complex matrices in actual water samples. In contrast, dual-mode detection can cross-reference and mutual correction, so that the results of on-site detection have higher accuracy and credibility [29,30]. Fluorescence method, like colorimetric method, also has the advantages of fast response and facile operation [31]. Meanwhile due to the rapid development of smartphones, which made them have more precise and clear and higher pixel camera function, it also made them feasible to be introduced into the development of analytical method. Recently, many works have been performed focusing on the exploitation of colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensors for trace determination of pollutants with the assistance of smartphones [32,33]. Liu et al. prepared a bifunctional metal-organic framework material (NH2-CuBDC) with high peroxidase-like activity by using Cu2+ as the metal node and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) as the organic linker. With the existence of H2O2, OPD can be oxidized into DAP with orange fluorescence and naked eye yellow, and the blue fluorescence of the nanozyme itself at 448 nm is quenched. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) can catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to thiocholine (TCh). The thiol groups in TCh tend to coordinate with Cu2+ in NH2-CuBDC to inhibit the catalytic activity of nanozymes. However, organophosphorus pesticides will inactivate AChE and lead to a decrease in TCh production, resulting in the recovery of enzyme-like activity. A colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensor for the detection of organophosphorus pesticides was constructed based on the color change of the generated solution and the change of the fluorescence signal. Shen et al. fabricated a new nanozyme by embedding tetraphenylethylene (TPE) into hollow CeO2 nanospheres (CeO2@TPE), which had blue luminescence, high peroxidase-like activity and had strong fluorescence emission at 441 nm. Interestingly, CH3SH can initiate the catalytic activity of ethanol oxidase and convert CH3SH into H2O2, which activates the peroxidase-like activity of CeO2@TPE, causing OPD converting into DAP which had an absorption peak at 425 nm and a strong fluorescence emission at 551 nm. Based on these, an enzyme cascade activation ratiometric fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode system was constructed to achieve rapid detection of CH3SH [34].

Although the colorimetric sensor based on nanozymes has the advantages of rapid response and high sensitivity for the detection of a single pollutant in the environment, it usually cannot achieve simultaneous detection of multiple targets. However, there are many pollutants having similar structures in the natural environment, which make it difficult to sensitively and effectively identify them. Therefore, it is very important to design and fabricate sensors for distinguishing multiple targets at the same time. In recent years, there are some reports on the discrimination of multiple pollutants of the same type or similar structure in the environment [35]. Xue et al. fabricated three-dimensional mesoporous carbon nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity by co-doping with metal Fe, Cu, N and P (FeCu/NPCs and Cu/NPCs), which can convert TMB into blue products (oxTMB). In the presence of AChE, the hydrolysis product of acetylcholine can inhibit the oxidation process of TMB. However, terpenoids can be used as the inhibitors of AChE to decrease the hydrolysis of ATCh. According to the inhibition difference of AChE by different terpenoids, an array sensor was constructed to distinguish a variety of terpenoids [36]. As a photoluminescence material, carbon dots have the advantages of easy preparation, low cost and low toxicity. Therefore, carbon dots show great application potential in sensing, bioimaging, disease treatment and so on [37]. In recent years, carbon dots have been reported to have enzyme-like activity due to their small size and rich active sites. Wang et al. synthesized N, P, S co-doped carbon dots (NPS–C) by one-step high-temperature pyrolysis. The doping of elements can improve the electron transfer channel between the nanozyme and the chromogenic substrate by promoting the redistribution of electrons, and further enhance the peroxidase-like activity [38]. Combining the enzyme-like activity of carbon dots with the unique fluorescence properties of carbon dots, a fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode sensor can be developed to detect the target analyte.

Herein, Cu and N co-doped carbon dots were loaded on g-C3N4 by facile hydrothermal method. The obtained g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Transmission electron microscopy and as on. A colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensing platform for the measurement of BDT was developed by using g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs as nanozyme and OPD as chromogenic substrate, and then the stability and feasibility of the sensing platform were verified. The array sensor is based on the difference of responses from the different target analytes to different chromogenic substrates, and each target leads to different degree of color change in the solution. By combining the colorimetric method with the array sensor, these colorimetric fingerprints can be obtained more intuitively, and the target can be distinguished by the obtained fingerprints of each analyte. Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) and principal component analysis (PCA) are often employed to distinguish multiple target analytes. Based on the above principles, a new array sensor was constructed with OPD, TMB and ABTS as chromogenic substrates, and six sulfur-containing compounds were quickly distinguished with the assistance of a smartphone.

Disodium edetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), Cu(NO3)2•3H2O, CH3COONa, Na3C6H5O7, melamine were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethyl benzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), 1,4-benzenendithiol (BDT), Na2S, thiourea, l-cysteine (L-cys), glutathione (GSH), 4-aminothiophenol (PATP) were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV mini-1280 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analysis (FTIR) was carried on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence measurements were obtained from an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were achieved on a JEM-2100 Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance Diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), Escalab 250XI (Thermo, USA) was employed to record full elemental spectra. The photos for analysis were taken using a nova 9 smartphone (Huawei, Shenzhen, China).

Firstly, 18.5 g melamine was weighed, and put into a muffle furnace, heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 3 °C/min, and maintained at 550 °C for 4 h. After naturally cooled to ambient temperature, the obtained yellow g-C3N4 bulk was ground into powder with an agate mortar, sonicated in deionized water for 2 h and then freeze-dried.

The obtained g-C3N4 (100 mg) was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water and sonicated for half an hour, then EDTA-Na2 (20.0 mg) and Cu(NO3)2 (15.7 mg) were added to the solution in turn. The above mixture was stirred for half an hour and then put into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooled to ambient temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The remained yellow solid was freeze-dried. Finally, g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were obtained, and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs with different loadings, hydrothermal temperatures and hydrothermal durations were also prepared according to the above steps for subsequent optimization and comparison.

Firstly, 160 μL g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and 1440 μL HAc-NaAc (0.2 M, pH 6.5) buffer solution were fortified into a centrifuge tube. Then, 200 μL BDT with different concentrations (0–200 μM), 100 μL OPD (4 mM), and 600 μL H2O2 (10 mM) were put into the centrifuge tube in turn, then the mixture was reacted at 35 °C for 25 min. After that, the absorption spectra of the solution were recorded utilizing a UV–visible spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

At the same time, 1 mL solution was taken from the above centrifuge tube and added to the 1.5 mL centrifuge tube. Photos were taken with a smartphone under visible light and ultraviolet light (254 nm, 16 W) with a vertical height of 30 cm and a horizontal distance of 40 cm. The photos were analyzed by the mobile phone software ‘colograb’ and the RGB values were derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: In order to distinguish six sulfur-containing compounds, an array sensor was constructed using three chromogenic substrates (OPD, TMB and ABTS). Specifically, 200 μL of different concentrations of sulfur-containing compounds and 160 μL g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were fully mixed and added to the HAc-NaAc buffer, and then 100 μL OPD (4 mM), TMB (4 mM) or ABTS (4 mM) were fortified to make the final volume of the solution 2.5 mL. The pH values of buffer solutions were pH 6.5, pH 5.0 and pH 4.0, respectively. After incubating at ambient temperature for 20 min, the color of the solution was photographed by a smartphone under visible light and the RGB values were exported by the mobile phone software. Different chromogenic substrates (OPD, TMB and ABTS) were used to act as different channels (channel 1, 2 and 3), each sulfur-containing compound was measured 3 times. Subsequently, the data matrixes (3 chromogenic substrates × 6 analytes × 3 replicates) were recorded and analyzed by hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) in SPSS Statistics 23 software. Finally, the 2D canonical score plots obtained with principal component analysis (PCA) were utilized to distinguish the six sulfur-containing compounds.

In order to verify whether the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were successfully synthesized, the surface morphology and elemental composition of the prepared g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were analyzed with TEM and EDS. From the TEM images shown in Fig. 1 A–C, it was clear that the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs possessed a clear layered structure The EDS spectrum shows (Fig. 1D) that the nanomaterial had Cu, N, C and O elements, and the mass ratios were 37.46 %, 21.52 %, 2.31 % and 38.69 %, respectively (Table S1), indicating that Cu, N-CDs had been successfully modified to g-C3N4.

The XRD patterns of Cu, N-CDs, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@Cu,N-CDs were shown in Fig. 1E. The peak of CDs at 21.7° corresponded to the (014) crystal plane of hydrazine hydrate copper (JCPDS card No.37-0532). The peaks of g-C3N4 at 13.0° and 27.6° correspond to (100) and (002) crystal planes [39], respectively. The XRD pattern of g-C3N4@Cu,N-CDs retained the (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4 at 27.6°, but the (014) crystal plane of the original carbon dots and the (100) crystal plane of g-C3N4 disappeared. Instead, a new peak was generated at 10.6°, corresponding to the (001) crystal plane of graphite, indicating that the modification of CDs changed the original structure of g-C3N4 during the secondary hydrothermal process [40].

The FTIR spectra of Cu, N-CDs, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were shown in Fig. 1F. The absorption at 3296 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of the triazine N–H bond, and the absorption at 3178 cm−1 was attributed to the = C–H stretching vibration. In the infrared spectrum of Cu, N-CDs, the peaks at 2979 cm−1 and 2902 cm−1 were due to the asymmetric stretching vibration of-CH2, which was derived from EDTA-2Na as carbon source. The absorption peaks at 1638 cm−1,1390 cm−1 and 1248 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CO/CN bond, C–O bond and C–N bond, respectively [41]. The peak at 811 cm−1 was a typical triazine respiratory vibration. A new peak appeared at 532 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, which was attributed to the Cu–O bond [42].

In order to understand the chemical bonds of the elements in g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs and the valence state of the elements, XPS tests were carried out. The full-scan spectrum (Fig. 2 A) revealed four characteristic binding energy peaks, which were located at 288.1 eV, 398.1 eV, 532.1 eV and 932.1 eV matching with C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p, respectively. The C 1s spectra of nanozymes were observed at 284.6, 286.0, and 287.8 eV, as shown in Fig. 2B, which could be ascribed to the CC, C–N/C–O, and CO groups, respectively. The peaks at 398.3 and 399.9 eV in the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) were assigned to the CN–C and N−(C)3 groups [33]. The Cu 2p spectra (Fig. 2D) can be deconvoluted into five different types of peaks at 933.2, 934.5, 942.9, 951.8, and 953.1 eV, which were assigned to Cu2+ 2p3/2, Cu+ 2p3/2, Cu 2p3/2 sat., Cu2+ 2p1/2 and Cu+ 2p1/2, respectively [43]. The above results indicated that g-C3N4@Cu,N-CDs had been successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs and the principle of colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 A, with the presence of only OPD and H2O2 in the solution, there was only one absorption peak at 289 nm which belonged to the absorption peak of OPD. When g-C3N4 or Cu, N-CDs was fortified to the above solution, a weak absorption peak was generated at 425 nm, which indicated that a small amount of OPD was oxidized to yellow product 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). That is to say, g-C3N4 and Cu, N-CDs both had weak catalytic activity. It was obvious that there was also no absorption peak only g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs in the solution. However, when OPD, H2O2 and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs coexisted in the solution, the peak at 289 nm basically disappeared, and two obvious absorption peaks at 259 nm and 425 nm were generated instead, which belonged to the absorption peak of DAP, indicating that g-C3N4@Cu,N-CDs had significant enzyme-like activity. In this experiment, the absorption peak intensity of 425 nm was used as the characteristic absorption peak of DAP.

For verifying the type of enzyme activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs and inhibiting effect of BDT, the absorption spectra with or without H2O2, g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs or BDT as well as EPR spectrum were measured. As exhibited in Fig. 3B there was no obvious absorption peak appearing at 425 nm, and the color of the solution was almost unchanged with no addition of H2O2 or g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs in the solution. With fortifying H2O2, the solution immediately became yellow, and the absorption spectrum had a significant absorption peak at 425 nm. The above phenomena indicated that g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs earned peroxidase-like activity rather than oxidase-like activity. In order to explore the types of free radicals produced during the oxidation process, isopropanol (IPA) and 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ) were used as capture agents for •OH and •O2 −, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, the relative activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs was significantly reduced after the addition of IPA, but there was no significant change after the addition of PBQ. The above results indicated that •OH played a major role in the oxidation process. Further the EPR spectrum of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + H2O2 solution confirmed the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs once again. As shown in Fig. S2, g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + H2O2 solution generated four typical peaks in the EPR spectrum, and the intensity ratio was about 1:2:2:1, indicating the generation of •OH, which accurately revealed that g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs catalyzed H2O2 to generate •OH through the peroxidase-like catalytic activity triggered by the Fenton-like reaction [44]. These results confirmed that g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs exhibited outstanding peroxidase-like activity. In addition, with fortifying BDT to the mixure, the yellow color of the solution became lighter, and the absorption intensity at 425 nm decreased, indicating that BDT could inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs to a certain extent.

The fluorescence 3D profiles of different solutions are shown in Fig. S3. When there was only nanozyme in the solution, the optimal excitation wavelength was at 250 nm and the maximum emission was at 362 nm (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B exhibits that the solution had no fluorescence emission in the range of 200–800 nm when there was only OPD in the solution. However, when both were present in the solution, the fluorescence of the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs was partially quenched at 362 nm and a new emission peak was generated at 297 nm (Fig. 4C). When g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2 were present in the solution, the emission peak intensity at 362 nm changed little, while the emission peak intensity at 297 nm increased significantly. Meanwhile, a new emission emerged at 585 nm, which was due to the characteristic emission of DAP, corresponding to three excitation wavelengths of 250 nm, 289 nm and 425 nm, respectively. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of nanozyme and DAP was set at 250 nm in the subsequent experiments. Further, BDT with a final concentration of 16.7 μM was added to the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs + OPD + H2O2 system. From Fig. 4E, it was apparent that the emission intensities at 297 nm and 585 nm were significantly reduced, while the emission intensity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs at 362 nm was slightly reduced. When the concentration of BDT increased to 167 μM, the emission intensities at three wavelengths were almost completely quenched (Fig. 4F).

The principle of smartphone-assisted colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT was exhibited in Scheme 1 . With the presence of H2O2 and g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs, abundant •OH radicals were generated while Cu+ and Cu2+ in Cu, N-CDs were converted to each other. g-C3N4 is a two-dimensional (2D) lamellar structure material with large specific surface area and highly delocalized π-conjugated system, which can accelerate the transfer of electrons. The generated •OH radicals can oxidize OPD into DAP. BDT has a certain reducibility, which will suppress the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs and reduce the oxidation of OPD, resulting in a decrease in DAP production. Therefore, the characteristic absorption intensity of PAD at 425 nm in UV–visible spectrum gradually decreased with the increase of BDT concentration. However, the emission intensity at 362 nm changed little with the rise of BDT concentration (Fig. S3), and it was selected as the built-in adjustment signal. The emission intensities at 297 nm and 585 nm were greatly affected by the concentration of BDT. The fluorescence intensities corresponding to the two emission wavelengths resulted in the same trend with the change of BDT concentration, and the fitting results were depicted in Fig. S4. Among them, the fluorescence emission at 297 nm was in the ultraviolet region, which could not be observed by the naked eye, while the fluorescence emission at 585 nm was orange and sensitive to the naked eye under ultraviolet light irradiation. Therefore, the fluorescence emission at 585 nm was finally utilized as the response signal. After fortifying BDT at 167 μM, the production of DAP was remarkably reduced, resulting in a remarkable decline of the emission intensity at 585 nm in the reaction system. From the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. S5), it can be clearly seen that the color of the solution changed from orange fluorescence to blue-purple fluorescence. Therefore, an atypical ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed with the fluorescence intensity ratio (F585nm/F362nm) as the quantitative signal. Compared with the change of DAP single-wavelength emission intensity to quantify the concentration of BDT, ratiometric fluorescence detection can reduce the interference caused by external conditions, thereby improving the accuracy and stability of the detection results. At the same time, under the irradiation of 254 nm ultraviolet lamp, the color change of generated fluorescent emission intensity and visible absorption intensity of the detection system was captured by smartphone, and the color was identified as RGB value by colorgrab software. According to the above principles, a smartphone-assisted portable dual-mode detection BDT visual sensing platform was constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For achieving the optimal enzyme activity of nanozymes, the synthesis of nanozymes (hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal time, loading amount) and reaction conditions (pH, concentration of OPD and H2O2, nanozyme concentration, reaction time, reaction temperature) were optimized. The optimization results were exhibited in Fig. S6A-H. The results demonstrated that the optimal performance was achieved with g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs derived under the parameters as follows, hydrothermal temperature, 200 °C; hydrothermal time, 12 h; mass ratio of CDs and g-C3N4,1:5 (CDs0.2); pH 6.5; OPD concentration, 0.16 mM; H2O2 concentration, 2.4 mM; nanozyme concentration, 32 μg/mL; reaction time, 25 min; reaction temperature, 35 °C. At the same time, the changes of fluorescence intensity of nanozymes prepared in different pH solutions were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the nanozyme achieved the peak fluorescence intensity with the excitation wavelength at 250 nm and the solution pH controlled at pH 6.5. This was as the same as the result of the optimization of the enzyme activity conditions, so the buffer solution (pH 6.5) was finally selected as the reaction matrix for the subsequent experiments.

Under the above optimal conditions, the steady-state kinetics of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs was studied. The Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated by Michaelis-Menten equation V=Vmax [S]/(KM + [S]), where V is the initial reaction rate, Vmax is the maximum reaction rate, [S] is the substrate concentration, and KM is the Michaelis-Menten constant. The smaller KM value indicates that the nanozyme has a stronger affinity with the substrate, and the larger Vmax value indicates that the enzymatic reaction has a faster reaction rate. As shown in Fig.S8, when TMB was used as the substrate, the KM and Vmax of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs were calculated to be 0.025 mM and 7.394 × 10−8 M s−1(Fig. S8A), respectively. When H2O2 was used as the substrate, the KM and Vmax of the nanozyme were calculated to be 0.383 mM and 28.163 × 10−8 M s−1(Fig. S8B), respectively. In addition, the KM and Vmax values of present work were compared with other previously reported nanozymes, and the results were presented in Table S2. The results showed that the g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs prepared in this work had higher affinity to the substrate and faster reaction rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT by spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of the mixed solution corresponding to various concentrations of BDT were given in Fig. 5 A. With increasing the concentration of BDT, the absorption intensity of generated DAP at 425 nm gradually decreased and the solution color gradually turned from yellow to colorless. In the range of 0.75–15.1 μM and 15.1–151 μM, the absorbance at 425 nm (A 425nm) showed a good linear correlation with BDT concentration (C). The linear equations were obtained as A425nm = −0.0145C + 1.0975 (0.75 ≤ C ≤ 15.1 μM) and A 425nm = −0.0019C + 0.8999 (15.1 ≤ C ≤ 151 μM) (Fig. 5B), respectively. The detection limit of colorimetric method was calculated to be 0.15 μM based on 3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of fifteen blank samples, and k is the slope of the established standard curve).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the reaction system containing various levels of BDT were recorded in Fig. 5C. Linear fitting was performed with the ratio of the emission intensities at 559 nm and 362 nm (F 585nm/F 362nm) as the ordinate and the DAP concentration as the abscissa, which demonstrated that good linearity was achieved between the ratio value and the concentration of BDT in the range of 0.33–10.0 μM and 10.0–60.0 μM. The linear equations were F 585nm/F 362nm = −0.0721C + 2.5867 (0.33 ≤ C ≤ 10.0 μM) and F
585nm
/F
362nm = −0.0169C + 1.9896 (10.0 ≤ C ≤ 60.0 μM) (Fig. 5D), respectively. The detection limit was 0.16 μM. The results above indicated that the established sensing system possessed a good quantitative detection performance for BDT in colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence dual mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode detection of BDT with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Along with spectrometric detection, the above solutions were photographed by smartphone under irradiation of visible light and ultraviolet light, respectively, and the RGB values were identified and derived by colorgrab software. The results showed that the value of G/(R + B) had a good linear correlation with the concentration of BDT. The linear equations under visible light irradiation were G/(R + B) = −0.0096C + 0.7209 (0.75 ≤ C ≤ 11.3 μM) and G/(R + B) = −0.0009C + 0.6265 (11.3 ≤ C ≤ 132 μM) (Fig. 6 A), respectively. Under the irradiation of UV lamp (254 nm), the solution color turned from bright orange to weak orange and last to weak blue. The linear equations under UV irradiation were G/(R + B) = −0.0250C + 0.8767 (0.33 ≤ C ≤ 10.0 μM) and G/(R + B) = −0.0051C + 0.6768 (10.0 ≤ C ≤ 60.0 μM), respectively. The detection limits of smartphone-assisted colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode for BDT were calculated to be 0.32 μM and 0.25 μM (Fig. 6B), respectively. The spectrometric detection and smartphone detection have similar variation trends and linear ranges, which indicated that the established smartphone detection platform could accurately quantify the concentration of BDT within a certain range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BDT in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The established sensor has exhibited its merits, and its application potential was validated with actual water samples. Three water samples collected from Diequan Spring, Kunming Lake and Huahai Lake in local region were utilized for analysis. The blank and spiked samples were analyzed with ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and smartphone, respectively. The results were presented in Table S3. It was apparent that satisfied spiked recoveries in the range of 96.8–104.4 % in different water samples with smartphone were achieved, which were very similar with that detected with ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy indicating that the smartphone assisted colorimetric sensor had exhibited good sensing performance and repeatability in the rapid detection of BDT in water. These results demonstrated that the smartphone assisted colorimetric sensor earned acceptable accuracy for the determination of BDT in actual water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual colorimetric discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental results showed that Na2S, thiourea, L-cys, GSH and PATP exhibited similar characteristic with BDT, which could more or less inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs. Therefore, according to the color change characteristics of the solution caused by the difference of the inhibition ability of these sulfur-contained compounds, a smartphone-assisted sensing array sensor for distinguishing six sulfur-containing compounds was designed and constructed. In order to clearly distinguish six sulfur-containing compounds, OPD, TMB and ABTS were used as the chromogenic substrates labelling as different channels, respectively. The pH optimization results of the nanozyme sensor system with TMB and ABTS as chromogenic substrates were shown in Figs. S9A and S9B. The TMB system was finally controlled at pH 5 with the buffer solution and ABTS system was controlled at pH 4 for the array sensor. It can be seen from Figs. S9C and S9D that when TMB and ABTS were used as chromogenic substrates, the two UV absorption curves showed obvious absorption peaks at 652 nm and 425 nm, which corresponded to the characteristic absorption peaks of TMB and ABTS oxidation products, respectively. At this time, the color of the solution showed blue and green visible to the naked eye. The color of the solution was captured with a smartphone, and the RGB value was derived. Finally, the G/(R + B) value was used as the colorimetric response of each channel. For each sulfur-containing compound (BDT, Na2S, thiourea, L-cys, GSH, PATP), three repeated tests were performed to generate a matrix of 6 sulfur-containing compounds × 3 channels × 3 replicates.

The measuring principle of the array sensor for sulfur-containing compounds resulted from their different inhibitory performance on the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs. By selecting different combinations of different chromogenic substrates, multi-channel information can be extracted from a single nanozyme for pattern recognition. As exhibited in Fig. 7 , the high-throughput recognition array photos of six sulfur-containing compounds (each, 40 μM) under three chromogenic substrates were identified as different RGB values by smartphone software, and each sulfur-containing compound had a specific colorimetric response mode. The software SPSS23 was used to test the feasibility of factor analysis. The KMO and Barlett test of sphericity results showed that the KMO value was larger than 0.5, and the significance was less than 0.05, indicating that the data was suitable for factor analysis. The training matrix has three principal components, of which the first two principal components are used to generate a 2D typical score plot.

As shown in Fig. 8 A, the six sulfur-containing compounds could be completely distinguished when the concentration was 40 μM. Each point represented a response pattern to a specific sulfur-containing compound, and these points were accurately located in their respective regions without crossover. Six distinct regions corresponded to BDT, Na2S, thiourea, L-cys, GSH and PATP, respectively. When the concentration of sulfur-containing compound was reduced to 4 μM and 0.4 μM, it was observed that the six regions were independent of each other and still did not cross, which could be completely distinguished by this array sensor (Fig. 8B and C). However, when the concentration of sulfur-containing compound was reduced to 0.1 μM, it was seen that the regions of sulfur-containing compounds except BDT overlapped and could not be distinguished (Fig. 8D). This may be due to the similar inhibition ability of the five targets to the three chromogenic substrates at low concentrations, resulting in similar color of the solution. The color reaction photos of six sulfur-containing compounds on three substrates at different concentrations were shown in Fig. S10.

The hierarchical cluster analysis of different concentrations of sulfur-containing compounds was shown in Fig. 8E–H. The numerical numbering of the ordinates represented the six sulfur compounds. Among them, No.1-3 represented BDT, No.4-6 represented Na2S, No.7-9 represented thiourea, No.10-12 represented L-cys, No.13-15 represented GSH, No.16-18 represented PATP. Similar with PCA results, the six sulfur-containing compounds were successfully distinguished at BDT concentrations of 40 μM, 4 μM and 0.4 μM, respectively. However, when the concentration of sulfur-containing compound was reduced to 0.1 μM, the remaining five sulfur-containing compounds, except BDT, were confused with each other and could not be successfully distinguished. The above results showed that the smartphone-based visual colorimetric sensor array constructed in this experiment could completely distinguish the six sulfur-containing compounds when their concentrations were low down to 0.4 μM.

For evaluating the recognition ability of the sensor array to the mixed solution, BDT and Na2S with various molar ratios (BDT: Na2S = 100 %: 0 %, 80 %: 20 %, 60 %: 40 %, 20 %: 80 %, 0 %: 100 %, total concentration, 0.4 μM) were studied. As shown in Fig. S11, 0.4 μM of BDT or Na2S or the mixture of these two compounds can easily be recognized by each other through the canonical score plot. Finally, in order to further validate the real discrimination performance of the nanozyme array sensor designed for six sulfur compounds, eighteen actual samples were obtained by spiking three kinds of actual water samples with each of the six sulfur-containing compounds at 4 μM. The 2D typical score diagram obtained by principal component analysis was shown in Fig. S12. It was found that there was no crossover between the six sulfur-containing compounds, indicating that they could be distinguished from each other. Subsequently, random fifteen among the above eighteen actual samples were taken out for blind sample analysis. The results showed that the fifteen actual samples could be successfully distinguished (Table S4). The above results showed that the smart phone-assisted array sensor constructed in this work possessed great potential practical application value.

Most sulfur-containing compounds have irritating odors, and long-term exposure will cause serious harm to the digestive system and nervous system of the human body. In this work, nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs with high-efficiency peroxidase-like activity was successfully designed and fabricated. A smartphone assisted colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode sensor based on nanozyme g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs was established based on the inhibition effect of sulfur-containing compounds on the peroxidase-like activity of g-C3N4@Cu, N-CDs. The constructed sensor exhibited excellent ability for accurate and rapid visual detection of BDT, and reliably distinguishing six sulfur-containing compounds at low level, and the discrimination of sulfides in actual water samples resulted acceptable results. This work provided a fast, efficient and portable method for the on-site detection of BDT and the discrimination of sulfur-containing compounds.

Linchun Nie: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liushan Jiang: Validation, Resources, Methodology. Shuangying Li: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Denghao Song: Methodology, Formal analysis. Guangyu Dong: Visualization, Investigation. Lutong Bu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Chunmao Chen: Validation, Resources. Qingxiang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

Environ. Sci. Technol.
